metrics.name: Wale
metrics.question: Will Matthew Wale be elected as the next Prime Minister of Solomon Islands following the May 2026 no-confidence vote?

reversal_search.why: every actor already holds a reversal-capable state

simulation_result.question: Will Matthew Wale be elected as the next Prime Minister of Solomon Islands following the May 2026 no-confidence vote?
limitations: forecast decomposition — grounded prior 0.1667 (counted n=2); simulation-conditional 0.1302 (resolved mass 1.00); headline 0.1302 via combiner_unavailable_range_only. Prior and simulation are reported separately and never blended by a fixed rule.
bounded omitted-state residual 0.738: the interval widens to [0.0, 0.8607] — private-state omissions are BOUNDS, never unknown-state worlds

no leakage-audited prior↔simulation reliability combiner is fitted — the headline is the mass-based recovery blend (resolved mass keeps its simulated conditional; unresolved mass takes the grounded prior); the two inputs are reported separately and never blended by a fixed rule

blueprint.causal_thesis: Matthew Wale, as opposition leader, seeks to convert his coalition's parliamentary majority into his own election as Prime Minister, but fluid coalition politics and rival candidates like John Agovaka may produce a compromise candidate, determining the outcome.

validation_failures_final.what: action vote_for_pm requires authority ['mp status'] that none of its actors hold

terminal_canonicalization.why: vote terminals read the tally directly

pruned.reason: ceremonial: no discretion over any surviving terminal pathway

solomon_islands_coalition_instability.claim: Solomon Islands coalition politics are notoriously fluid; the leader of a no-confidence motion does not always become PM, as coalition negotiations can produce compromise candidates.

provenance.definition: Solomon Islands coalition politics are notoriously fluid; the leader of a no-confidence motion does not always become PM, as coalition negotiations can produce compromise candidates.
provenance.level_fallback_reason: all levels sparse (total 2 usable case(s)); rate is wide by construction

cases.description: In 2019, after a no-confidence motion against Prime Minister Rick Hou, the opposition coalition elected Manasseh Sogavare as PM, not the motion's leader.
cases.basis_quote: Manasseh Sogavare was elected prime minister by parliament on 24 April 2019, replacing Rick Hou, after a no-confidence vote.

cases.description: In 2014, after the general election, a coalition led by the Democratic Alliance Party elected Manasseh Sogavare as PM, bypassing other candidates.
cases.basis_quote: Manasseh Sogavare was elected prime minister by parliament on 9 December 2014, heading a coalition government.

china_taiwan_diplomatic_rivalry.claim: Solomon Islands' shift to China in 2019 and subsequent tensions with Taiwan create external pressure on leadership choices, affecting coalition stability.

provenance.definition: Solomon Islands' shift to China in 2019 and subsequent tensions with Taiwan create external pressure on leadership choices, affecting coalition stability.
provenance.level_fallback_reason: all levels sparse (total 1 usable case(s)); rate is wide by construction

cases.description: In 2019, Prime Minister Manasseh Sogavare switched diplomatic recognition from Taiwan to China, leading to internal political turmoil and a no-confidence vote.
cases.basis_quote: Solomon Islands switches diplomatic recognition from Taiwan to China, prompting political crisis.

economic_dependence_on_aid.claim: Solomon Islands relies heavily on foreign aid, particularly from China and Australia, which influences political alignments and coalition formation.

provenance.definition: Solomon Islands relies heavily on foreign aid, particularly from China and Australia, which influences political alignments and coalition formation.
provenance.level_fallback_reason: all levels sparse (total 1 usable case(s)); rate is wide by construction

cases.description: In 2021, the Solomon Islands government accepted a security pact with China, reflecting aid-driven alignment.
cases.basis_quote: Solomon Islands signs security pact with China, deepening ties amid aid dependence.

matthew_wale.quantity: Matthew Wale is elected Prime Minister

provenance.definition: Matthew Wale is formally elected or appointed as Prime Minister of Solomon Islands and sworn in by the Governor-General.
provenance.inclusion_criteria: Official announcement by Solomon Islands Government or credible news reporting (ABC News Australia, RNZ, etc.) that Matthew Wale has been elected and sworn in as Prime Minister.
provenance.exclusion_criteria: Any other individual elected; no election by August 1, 2026; Wale not sworn in.
provenance.level_fallback_reason: all levels sparse (total 1 usable case(s)); rate is wide by construction

cases.description: Matthew Wale was elected as Leader of the Opposition in 2019, but never became PM in prior no-confidence attempts.
cases.basis_quote: Matthew Wale elected as Leader of the Opposition, but not Prime Minister.

frederick_kologeto.quantity: Frederick Kologeto is elected Prime Minister

provenance.definition: Frederick Kologeto is formally elected or appointed as Prime Minister and sworn in.
provenance.inclusion_criteria: Official announcement or credible reporting that Frederick Kologeto has been elected and sworn in as Prime Minister.
provenance.exclusion_criteria: Any other individual elected; no election by deadline.
provenance.level_fallback_reason: all levels sparse (total 1 usable case(s)); rate is wide by construction

cases.description: Frederick Kologeto was a candidate for Prime Minister in 2019 but lost to Manasseh Sogavare.
cases.basis_quote: Frederick Kologeto was a candidate for prime minister but was not elected.

jeremiah_manele.quantity: Jeremiah Manele is re-elected Prime Minister

provenance.definition: Jeremiah Manele is re-elected as Prime Minister after the no-confidence vote.
provenance.inclusion_criteria: Official announcement or credible reporting that Jeremiah Manele has been re-elected and sworn in as Prime Minister.
provenance.exclusion_criteria: Any other individual elected; no election by deadline.
provenance.level_fallback_reason: all levels sparse (total 1 usable case(s)); rate is wide by construction

cases.description: Jeremiah Manele was elected Prime Minister in 2024.
cases.basis_quote: Jeremiah Manele elected prime minister in 2024.

cases.description: Jeremiah Manele ousted via no-confidence on May 7, 2026.
cases.basis_quote: Jeremiah Manele ousted via no-confidence vote.

outcome_reference_class.quantity: Matthew Wale is elected Prime Minister

provenance.definition: Matthew Wale is formally elected or appointed as Prime Minister of Solomon Islands and sworn in by the Governor-General on or after May 7, 2026.
provenance.inclusion_criteria: Official announcement by Solomon Islands Government or credible news reporting (ABC News Australia, RNZ, etc.) that Matthew Wale has been elected and sworn in as Prime Minister.
provenance.exclusion_criteria: Any other individual elected; no election by August 1, 2026; Wale not sworn in.
provenance.level_fallback_reason: all levels sparse (total 2 usable case(s)); rate is wide by construction

cases.description: In 2019, after a no-confidence vote, Manasseh Sogavare was elected PM, not the opposition leader.
cases.basis_quote: Manasseh Sogavare elected prime minister after no-confidence vote, not opposition leader.

cases.description: In 2014, after general election, Manasseh Sogavare elected PM from coalition.
cases.basis_quote: Manasseh Sogavare elected prime minister from coalition.

parliament_of_solomon_islands.consequence_of_nonparticipation: If no new PM elected by August 1, 2026, the question resolves No.

weight_invariant.rule: every weight traces to a counted reference class or is explicit unknown mass; no qualitative label is mapped to a number

reversal_search.why: every actor already holds a reversal-capable state

john_agovaka.residual_provenance: uncovered feasible option(s) ['frederick kologeto', 'jeremiah manele', 'john agovaka'] with no counted cases — bounded per option

opposition_coalition_mps.residual_provenance: uncovered feasible option(s) ['abstain', 'frederick kologeto', 'jeremiah manele'] with no counted cases — bounded per option

matthew_wale.claim: Matthew Wale believes he has secured a secret commitment from China to provide substantial aid and diplomatic support if he becomes Prime Minister, which he expects will sway undecided MPs in the coalition to back him.
beliefs: China's economic leverage is decisive in Solomon Islands politics.
The no-confidence vote succeeded because Manele lost Chinese support.
His own coalition is unified and will hold.
goals: Secure the premiership to implement a pro-China foreign policy.
Ensure continued Chinese aid flows to maintain stability.
matthew_wale.pressures: High pressure from Chinese diplomats to deliver a quick transition and from domestic allies expecting patronage.
stances: Pro-China alignment in the diplomatic rivalry with Taiwan.
Pragmatic acceptance of Chinese infrastructure loans.
historical_case_refs: Solomon Islands switched recognition from Taiwan to China in 2019 under Prime Minister Sogavare.
China provided rapid aid after the 2021 riots.
distinguishing_observations: Wale would accelerate negotiations with China for new projects.
He would sideline pro-Taiwan figures in his cabinet.
matthew_wale.action_if_state: Wale actively lobbies coalition MPs with promises of Chinese-funded development projects in their constituencies, and privately assures Chinese officials of continued alignment.

matthew_wale.claim: Matthew Wale privately fears that the coalition backing him is fragile and that rival factions, possibly backed by Taiwan or Australia, are plotting to defect and elect a different candidate.
beliefs: The no-confidence vote was a temporary alliance, not a stable majority.
External actors (Australia, Taiwan) are actively working to undermine him.
His own party lacks the numbers to govern alone.
goals: Prevent defections by offering ministerial posts and policy concessions.
Delay the prime ministerial vote to buy time for negotiations.
matthew_wale.pressures: Intense pressure from coalition partners demanding cabinet positions and policy commitments; risk of losing the vote if any MP defects.
stances: Cautious and conciliatory toward potential defectors.
Open to compromise on foreign policy to keep coalition together.
historical_case_refs: Solomon Islands has seen frequent no-confidence motions and coalition shifts, e.g., 2017 when Sogavare survived a motion
distinguishing_observations: Wale would seek a secret ballot to reduce defection risk.
He might offer the deputy premiership to a rival faction leader.
matthew_wale.action_if_state: Wale engages in intense behind-the-scenes negotiations, offering key posts to potential defectors and possibly delaying the parliamentary vote to secure commitments.

matthew_wale.claim: Matthew Wale believes he does not have enough votes to win and expects to lose the prime ministerial vote, so he is preparing to concede and position himself for a future leadership bid or retirement.
beliefs: The coalition is actually controlled by Frederick Kologeto or another rival.
His long tenure as opposition leader has made him too many enemies.
The no-confidence vote was a maneuver by others, not a path for him.
goals: Avoid a humiliating defeat by withdrawing gracefully.
Preserve his political capital for a future opportunity.
Secure a safe exit, possibly a diplomatic post.
matthew_wale.pressures: Pressure from his own party to step aside and from family to leave politics; media scrutiny of his potential loss.
stances: Resigned and pragmatic about the outcome.
Willing to cooperate with the new government if treated fairly.
historical_case_refs: Opposition leaders in Solomon Islands have often been sidelined after no-confidence votes, e.g., Manasseh Sogavare's per
Wale himself lost previous leadership contests.
distinguishing_observations: Wale would not actively campaign and might skip the vote.
He would issue a statement conceding and calling for unity.
matthew_wale.action_if_state: Wale privately informs his closest allies that he will not stand for prime minister, and begins discussions about a transition of opposition leadership or a role abroad.

john_agovaka.claim: John Agovaka believes Matthew Wale is a weak candidate who will be forced into a power-sharing deal with Manele loyalists, making Wale's premiership unstable and short-lived.
beliefs: Wale lacks the internal party discipline to command a stable majority
Manele's faction will extract key ministerial posts in exchange for support
A Wale government will collapse within months, triggering another vote
goals: Position himself as the unity candidate for a future stable government
Avoid openly backing Wale to preserve his own coalition options
Signal to foreign donors that he can deliver continuity
john_agovaka.pressures: Constituents fear economic disruption from prolonged instability; China and Australia both seek a predictable partner.
stances: Publicly neutral but privately critical of Wale's leadership
Advocates for a 'government of national unity' that includes Manele allies
historical_case_refs: Solomon Islands 2021 no-confidence cycle where Sogavare returned after short-lived replacement
distinguishing_observations: Agovaka has not publicly endorsed Wale despite being a senior opposition figure
john_agovaka.action_if_state: Agovaka will quietly lobby MPs to impose conditions on Wale's candidacy, demanding a power-sharing agreement that includes Manele's faction, and may abstain from the vote to avoid being tied to a fail

john_agovaka.claim: John Agovaka believes Matthew Wale has secured a solid majority with a clear reform mandate, sidelining Manele's faction entirely.
beliefs: Wale has locked in 28+ votes from opposition and disaffected Manele MPs
The no-confidence vote revealed irreversible fractures in Manele's coalition
Wale will appoint a cabinet free of Manele loyalists
goals: Secure a senior cabinet position (e.g., Finance or Foreign Affairs)
Be seen as a key architect of the new government to boost his own PM prospects later
Align with Wale's expected pro-China tilt to ensure aid flows
john_agovaka.pressures: Must demonstrate loyalty to the winning coalition to secure a portfolio; China expects a reliable partner in the new government.
stances: Publicly enthusiastic about Wale's leadership
Emphasizes continuity of diplomatic relations with China
historical_case_refs: Sogavare's 2019 clean sweep after defections from opposition
distinguishing_observations: Agovaka was one of the first MPs to publicly congratulate Wale after the no-confidence vote
john_agovaka.action_if_state: Agovaka will actively campaign for Wale among undecided MPs, offer to serve as his campaign manager for the PM vote, and position himself as Wale's natural deputy.

john_agovaka.claim: John Agovaka believes the no-confidence vote has created a deadlock where neither Wale nor any other candidate can secure a majority, opening a path for himself as a compromise PM.
beliefs: Wale's coalition is fragile and may fracture before the vote
Manele's faction will block Wale but cannot unite behind an alternative
Agovaka is seen as a moderate acceptable to both China and Australia
goals: Secretly lobby MPs from both camps to support him as a consensus candidate
Delay the PM vote to allow his own coalition-building to mature
Portray himself as the only leader who can prevent a prolonged crisis
john_agovaka.pressures: International donors are alarmed by instability; Agovaka must balance Chinese and Australian expectations while appearing independent.
stances: Publicly claims he is not a candidate while privately negotiating
Criticizes both Wale and Manele as divisive figures
historical_case_refs: Danny Philip's surprise election in 2010 after deadlock between major candidates
distinguishing_observations: Agovaka has made no public endorsement and has been unusually quiet since the no-confidence vote
john_agovaka.action_if_state: Agovaka will use backchannel negotiations to peel off MPs from both Wale and Manele camps, propose a delay in the PM vote citing procedural concerns, and eventually put his own name forward as a unity

frederick_kologeto.claim: Frederick Kologeto privately believes that Matthew Wale is the only candidate who can unite the coalition and secure enough international aid, despite personal rivalry.
beliefs: Matthew Wale's long experience as Opposition Leader makes him acceptable to both pro-China and pro-Taiwan factions.
Wale's election will stabilize aid flows from Australia and the West, which are critical for the economy.
goals: Ensure the coalition holds together to avoid immediate snap elections.
Maintain influence over key ministries in a Wale-led government.
frederick_kologeto.pressures: Economic dependence on aid and the need to balance China-Taiwan rivalry force a pragmatic choice.
stances: Support Matthew Wale for Prime Minister.
historical_case_refs: Similar coalition compromises in Pacific island nations (e.g., Papua New Guinea 2019) where opposition leaders were elec
distinguishing_observations: Kologeto does not put himself forward as a candidate.
He actively lobbies coalition members to back Wale.
frederick_kologeto.action_if_state: Kologeto will publicly endorse Matthew Wale and use his party's votes to secure Wale's election, while negotiating for key cabinet posts.

frederick_kologeto.claim: Frederick Kologeto privately believes he can become Prime Minister himself by rallying a faction of the coalition against Wale.
beliefs: His role in tabling the no-confidence motion gives him legitimacy as a reformer.
A split among coalition members can be exploited to position himself as a consensus candidate.
goals: Become Prime Minister to implement his party's agenda.
Reduce dependence on foreign aid by strengthening ties with China.
frederick_kologeto.pressures: Coalition instability and the need to secure a majority before the August 1 deadline.
stances: Oppose Matthew Wale's candidacy.
Promote himself as an alternative.
historical_case_refs: Manasseh Sogavare's return to power in 2019 after a no-confidence vote, leveraging coalition splits.
distinguishing_observations: Kologeto privately meets with smaller party leaders to build support.
He delays endorsing any candidate publicly.
frederick_kologeto.action_if_state: Kologeto will secretly negotiate with defectors from Wale's camp and put his own name forward for Prime Minister, risking a prolonged vote.

frederick_kologeto.claim: Frederick Kologeto privately believes Matthew Wale's election would lead to a purge of his allies and loss of influence, so he will sabotage the process.
beliefs: Wale sees Kologeto as a rival and will exclude him from power.
A failed vote or delay could trigger a snap election where Kologeto's party might gain seats.
goals: Prevent Wale from becoming Prime Minister by any means.
Force a dissolution of parliament to improve his party's position.
frederick_kologeto.pressures: Coalition instability and the risk of being sidelined in a Wale government.
stances: Vote against Wale in every round.
Encourage coalition members to abstain or vote for a minor candidate.
historical_case_refs: Derek Sikua's failed no-confidence bid in 2010 led to a prolonged political crisis and eventual dissolution.
distinguishing_observations: Kologeto raises procedural objections to delay the vote.
He leaks damaging information about Wale to the media.
frederick_kologeto.action_if_state: Kologeto will instruct his party to vote against Wale and will attempt to convince other coalition members to do the same, aiming to deadlock parliament.

jeremiah_manele.claim: Jeremiah Manele believes he can secure a return to power by brokering a secret deal with Matthew Wale's coalition, offering key ministerial posts and policy concessions in exchange for being reinstated as Prime Minister after a short interim period.
beliefs: Coalition politics in Solomon Islands are fluid and personal loyalty is secondary to patronage
Matthew Wale's coalition is fragile and can be split with sufficient incentives
goals: Regain the Prime Ministership
Maintain influence over foreign policy, especially regarding China
jeremiah_manele.pressures: Intense lobbying from China-aligned business interests and personal ambition to avoid political obscurity
stances: Opposes any permanent shift away from China recognition
Willing to temporarily support Wale if it leads to his own return
historical_case_refs: Solomon Islands 2021 no-confidence vote where Manasseh Sogavare returned after being ousted
distinguishing_observations: Manele has not publicly conceded defeat and is known for back-channel negotiations
jeremiah_manele.action_if_state: Manele privately approaches key MPs in Wale's coalition with offers of cabinet positions and development funds, while publicly maintaining a neutral stance to avoid appearing desperate.

jeremiah_manele.claim: Jeremiah Manele accepts his ouster as final and plans to leave Solomon Islands politics permanently, seeking a diplomatic post or academic position abroad to avoid potential legal repercussions from his tenure.
beliefs: The no-confidence vote reflects irreversible loss of parliamentary support
Staying in Solomon Islands would lead to political persecution or marginalization
goals: Secure personal safety and financial stability
Avoid prosecution for alleged corruption during his term
jeremiah_manele.pressures: Fear of legal action from the new government and loss of political allies
stances: Resigned to the outcome
Will not actively oppose the new government
historical_case_refs: Former Prime Minister Rick Houenipwela left politics after losing a no-confidence vote in 2019
distinguishing_observations: Manele has been quiet in public since the vote, and his family is reportedly making travel arrangements
jeremiah_manele.action_if_state: Manele quietly resigns his parliamentary seat and applies for a visiting fellowship at an Australian university, while instructing his lawyers to negotiate an exit deal with the new government.

jeremiah_manele.claim: Jeremiah Manele believes he can prevent Matthew Wale from becoming Prime Minister by launching a constitutional challenge to the no-confidence vote, arguing procedural irregularities, and forcing a fresh election that he could win.
beliefs: The no-confidence vote was procedurally flawed
The judiciary is sympathetic to his faction
goals: Invalidate the no-confidence vote
Trigger a snap election where he can campaign as a victim of a coup
jeremiah_manele.pressures: Need to act quickly before Wale consolidates power; support from China-aligned legal advisors
stances: Aggressively opposes Wale's candidacy
Seeks to delegitimize the parliamentary process
historical_case_refs: 2017 constitutional crisis in Solomon Islands where a no-confidence vote was challenged in court
distinguishing_observations: Manele has retained a prominent constitutional lawyer and is gathering affidavits from MPs about alleged bribery
jeremiah_manele.action_if_state: Manele files an urgent injunction in the High Court to halt the Prime Ministerial election, while rallying his remaining supporters to boycott the vote and demand a fresh election.

opposition_coalition_mps.claim: Matthew Wale is seen as the only leader who can hold the fragile six-party coalition together and secure the premiership, despite internal rivalries.
beliefs: Wale's long tenure as opposition leader gives him the necessary experience and cross-party respect.
Any other candidate would cause defections and hand victory to a Manele loyalist.
goals: Ensure Wale is elected Prime Minister in the upcoming vote.
Maintain coalition unity through the election process.
opposition_coalition_mps.pressures: High pressure from party whips and constituency expectations to deliver a stable government quickly.
stances: Strongly support Wale's candidacy publicly and privately.
Oppose any last-minute alternative candidates from within the coalition.
supporting_evidence_ids: Matthew Wale was instrumental in the legal challenges that forced the no-confidence vote.
historical_case_refs: Solomon Islands 2014 coalition government under Sogavare, where a unified opposition candidate secured premiership.
distinguishing_observations: Wale is the only candidate publicly endorsed by all six coalition parties so far.
opposition_coalition_mps.action_if_state: Vote unanimously for Wale and lobby any wavering MPs to stay in line.

opposition_coalition_mps.claim: A significant faction within the coalition distrusts Wale due to past broken promises and is secretly negotiating to back a dark horse candidate (e.g., Frederick Kologeto) for Prime Minister.
beliefs: Wale cannot be trusted to share power or resources equitably.
A different leader would better serve the faction's regional or personal interests.
goals: Install a Prime Minister loyal to the faction, possibly Kologeto or another MP.
Prevent Wale from gaining full control of the government.
opposition_coalition_mps.pressures: Pressure from party elders and donors to secure ministerial positions and policy concessions.
stances: Publicly maintain support for Wale to avoid coalition collapse.
Privately work to undermine Wale's candidacy and rally votes for an alternative.
supporting_evidence_ids: The no-confidence motion was tabled by Frederick Kologeto, indicating his ambition.
historical_case_refs: Solomon Islands 2017 coalition infighting that led to a surprise Prime Minister election.
distinguishing_observations: Kologeto has not explicitly ruled himself out as a candidate.
opposition_coalition_mps.action_if_state: Vote for the alternative candidate in the secret ballot, while publicly claiming to support Wale.

opposition_coalition_mps.claim: Some coalition MPs believe the no-confidence vote was a tactical move to extract concessions, and they are open to a power-sharing deal that returns Manele or a close ally to the premiership in exchange for key portfolios.
beliefs: Manele's ouster was about renegotiating terms, not regime change.
A deal with Manele's camp ensures stability and continued aid flows.
goals: Secure ministerial positions or policy commitments for their constituencies.
Avoid a prolonged political crisis that could harm the economy.
opposition_coalition_mps.pressures: Pressure from business interests and foreign donors (e.g., China, Australia) to ensure a stable government.
stances: Publicly advocate for a new Prime Minister from the coalition.
Privately signal willingness to support a Manele-aligned candidate if conditions are met.
supporting_evidence_ids: Economic dependence on aid and china_taiwan_diplomatic_rivalry create incentives for continuity.
historical_case_refs: Solomon Islands 2021 no-confidence vote where the ousted PM returned after coalition realignment.
distinguishing_observations: Manele's camp has not conceded defeat and is lobbying MPs.
opposition_coalition_mps.action_if_state: Vote for a compromise candidate aligned with Manele, breaking the coalition's public stance.

aliases_no: any other candidate receives at least 26 votes, or no candid

deliberations.note: I need to gauge actual support before committing to a vote that could expose my weakness. A public vote for myself now risks revealing I lack the numbers, emboldening Wale and Manele to close ranks. B

coalescer.randomness_rule: content-addressed (state/context-seeded) draws — equal keys share the future transition law by construction; independent streams carry a tag that refuses the merge

engine_primary.weight_source: counted_reference_class_posteriors (no qualitative label is mapped to a number anywhere in this engine); represented states carry the full branch mass — omitted-state residuals are bounded interval-wideners, never unknown-state worlds

skipped_reasons: no deterministic trigger fired: primary stable, no verified evidence conflict, no reversal-capable disputed assumption (imagination alone is not a trigger)

replicate_policy.reason: behavioral replicates not requested (deterministic execution: content-addressed draws make replicate 0 exhaustive)

provenance.quantity: Matthew Wale is elected Prime Minister
provenance.fallback_reason: all levels sparse (total 2 usable case(s)); rate is wide by construction

notes: no leakage-audited reliability combiner is fitted — prior and simulation are reported separately with the feasible combined range; no fixed blend is applied and Lean V2 must not become default on this basis

consequences.boundary_contract: delivery mechanics precompiled; interpretation/persuasion/reaction remain branch-local actor decisions

vote_for_pm.description: Each MP casts a vote for a candidate in the PM election.
vote_for_pm.validation: MP must be present and eligible to vote.
